titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Jan van Raay Photographs of Art Strike Protests
Raay (Jan van) Photographs of Art Strike Protests
titlestmt.author: by Brooke McManus

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.3830463

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday November 28, 2024 at 01:06

did.unittitle: Jan van Raay photographs of art strike protests

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The collection contains black-and-white photographs by Jan van Raay documenting strikes, demonstrations, protests, sit-ins, happenings, and other events in New York City, primarily at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) and the Metropolitan Museum of Art. Photographs depict actions including the Art Strike against Racism, Sexism, Repression, War; the PASTA MoMA union strike; demonstrations and marches in support of the Panther 21, Black and Puerto Rican artists, and abortion rights; and The People's Flag Show, an exhibition at Judson Memorial Church organized by Guerrilla Art Action Group (GAAG) co-founders Jon Hendricks and Jean Toche, and Faith Ringgold, a Black activist and artist, who were subsequently arrested for flag desecration and became known as the Judson Three.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research

bioghist.head: Jan van Raay (1942-)
bioghist.p: Jan van Raay is an American photographer, painter, and performance artist. She is known for photographing protests in New York City in the 1970s, as well as her portraits of public figures including Yoko Ono and John Lennon. Raay was born in Brooklyn, New York, and currently resides in Portland, Oregon.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection contains black-and-white photographs by Jan van Raay documenting strikes, demonstrations, protests, sit-ins, happenings, and other events in New York City, primarily at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) and the Metropolitan Museum of Art. Photographs depict actions including the Art Strike against Racism, Sexism, Repression, War; the PASTA MoMA union strike; demonstrations and marches in support of the Panther 21, Black and Puerto Rican artists, and abortion rights; and The People's Flag Show, an exhibition at Judson Memorial Church organized by Guerrilla Art Action Group (GAAG) co-founders Jon Hendricks and Jean Toche, and Faith Ringgold, a Black activist and artist, who were subsequently arrested for flag desecration and became known as the Judson Three.

The bulk of the collection documents the activities of GAAG, and included are copies of the group's manifestos, action scripts, open letters, internal memos, and other papers, such as letters written on behalf of Black activist Angela Davis during her imprisonment. Many photographs of protests and meetings prominently feature groups such as the Art Workers Coalition, Women Artists in Revolution, Ad Hoc Women Artists Committee, and Black Emergency Cultural Coalition. Some of the individuals pictured include Raay herself; GAAG co-founder Poppy Johnson; artists Takis, Tom Lloyd, Lil Picard, Roy Lichtenstein, Raphael Montañez Ortiz, Kate Millett, and Alice Neel; Black feminist lawyer Florynce Kennedy; attorney and American Civil Liberties Union Director William Kunstler; activist Abbie Hoffman; and MoMA Director John Hightower. Materials date from 1969 to 1974.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Antiquariat Guenter Linke on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2018.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Jan van Raay Photographs of Art Strike Protests. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections as they are acquired, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
This collection received a basic level of processing, including rehousing and minimal organization.

Information included in the Description of Papers note and Collection Contents section is drawn from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Folder titles appearing in the contents list below are often based on those provided by the creator or previous custodian. Titles have not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases. Otherwise, folder titles are supplied by staff during initial processing.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Jan van Raay Photographs of Art Strike Protests is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two series: I. Photographs, 1969-1972. II. Guerrilla Art Action Group Papers, 1974.

subject.part: Black Panthers Trial, New York, N.Y., 1970-1971

corpname.part: Ad Hoc Committee of Women Artists
Metropolitan Museum of Art (New York, N.Y.)
Museum of Modern Art (New York, N.Y.)

did.unittitle: Photographs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of photographs by Jan van Raay documenting strikes, marches, sit-ins, protests, and other actions by artists and activist groups in New York City. A number of photographs show activities involving the Guerrilla Art Action Group and Art Workers Coalition at MoMA, such as the Art Strike against Racism, Sexism, Repression, War (Art Strike); the anti-Vietnam War "Memorial Service for Dead Babies" in front of Picasso's Guernica ; demonstration in support of a study center for Black and Puerto Rican art at the museum; protest against New York Governor Nelson Rockefeller's response to the Attica prison uprising; and the Professional and Administrative Staff Association of the Museum of Modern Art (PASTA MoMA) strike. Raay photographed the Art Strike sit-in at the Metropolitan Museum of Art, as well as protests of the museum's planned Lehman Wing and expansion into Central Park. Also depicted are a march against New York State abortion laws; a courthouse demonstration in support of the Panther 21; and a public hearing on the question, "Are Museums Relevant to Women?" The series contains photographs of The People's Flag Show at Judson Memorial Church and organizers Faith Ringgold, Jean Toche, and Jon Hendricks, as well as meetings and rallies in support of the Judson Three after their arrest for flag desecration. Additionally included are images of performance art by Lil Picard. Most folders contain photocopied descriptions of the photographs by Jon Hendricks.

did.unittitle: Guerrilla Art Action Group so called "Malevich Action" at Museum of Modern Art
Guerrilla Art Action Group "memorial service for dead babies" at Museum of Modern Art in front of Picasso's Guernica
Art Workers Coalition with Guerrilla Art Action Group members demonstration and protest at Museum of Modern Art with And Babies poster
Guerrilla Art Action Group action at American Institute of Architects
[Art Workers Coalition meeting, demonstration]
Guerrilla Art Action Group action at American Institute of Architects conference at Cooper Union, New York City

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Note accompanying photographs: "For this action GAAG was joined by other artists in its realization. (Jon Hendricks was sick and didn't take part.) The AIA had been cooperating with the American Bar Ass. in the design of isolation [booths] for court rooms to control what defendants were able to say. Later AIA drop[p]ed the project."

did.unittitle: Lil Picard performing her anti-pollution event
[Meeting of the Art Workers Coalition with MoMA Director John Hightower]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Note accompanying photographs: "This was a meeting of the Art Workers Coalition at which John Hightower the new director of the Museum of Modern Art talked. There are a number of photographs here of GAAG (Johnson, Toche, Hendricks) questioning Hightower."

did.unittitle: Times Square, New York, anti-war action by Jon Hendricks and his students
Lil Picard anti-pollution event, New York City
[Art Workers Coalition meeting planning abortion rights demonstration]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes images of Jean Toche, Tom Lloyd (Black Emergency Cultural Coalition), and Takis (Art Workers Coalition). Note accompanying photographs: "Poppy Johnson of Ad Hoc Women Artists presented her work 'Is this uterus the property of N. Y. state?'"

did.unittitle: Guerrilla Art Action Group and Art Workers Coalition action in support of the first postal workers' strike
[Women Artists in Revolution and abortion rights march]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes an image of a mannequin wearing an apron with the slogan "Is this uterus the property of N. Y. state?"

did.unittitle: [Abortion rights march]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Note accompanying photographs: "Many groups participated including Women Artists in Revolution (W.A.R.), Ad Hoc Women Artists, G.A.A.G.; Poppy Johnson had made apron that people wore saying 'Is this uterus the property of N. Y. state?' Jon Hendricks did a short action."

did.unittitle: [Abortion rights rally]
One of the earliest meetings at Art Workers Coalition for what would become: The People's Flag Show
John Hightower addressing members of the Art Workers Coalition, New York State Council on the Arts, and others
Symposium on art and politics at Museum of Modern Art?

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photograph of MoMA Director John Hightower, Roy Lichtenstein, Alex Gross, Raphael Montañez Ortiz, and Congressman Ed Koch.

did.unittitle: R.F.S.S.B [Republic of the Federal Socialist States of Belgium] and government in exile

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Note accompanying photographs: "Jean Toche fast, and press conference at Judson Memorial Church demanding release of Herman Claeys."

did.unittitle: [Black and Puerto Rican Emergency Cultural Coalition, Guerrilla Art Action Group, and Art Workers Coalition protest at MoMA]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The action supported a campaign by Black and Puerto Rican artists to implement programs at MoMA relevant to their communities. Among the demands were creation of a Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. - Pedro Albizu Campos Study Center for Black and Puerto Rican Culture.

did.unittitle: Media students demonstration against CBS; Guerrilla Art Action Group action, arrest, and action
[Art Workers Coalition meeting at New York University?]
First meeting of Art Strike after mass meeting
Art Strike meeting at 727 B'way, N.Y.C; [Robert] Morris/[Poppy] Johnson chairpersons
Art Strike demonstration and closing of Metropolitan Museum of Art
Art Strike taking over the stage by the artists at the opening of the American Association of Museums Convention
Art Strike takeover and speeches at American Associations of Museum Convention
Art Strike confrontation with American Association of Museums meeting
[American Association of Museums meeting]
Possibly at one of our many confrontations with the American Association of Museums [and another unidentified event]
[American Association of Museums meeting]
Art Workers Coalition demonstration against the Lehman Wing at the Metropolitan Museum of Art
Governor Nelson Rockefeller addressing American Association of Museums conference and confrontation with artists of Art Strike
Art Strike demonstration at Museum of Modern Art
Art Strike demonstration at Museum of Modern Art by Women Artists in Revolution?
Art Strike manifestation at Museum of Modern Art
Art Strike presenting demands to Museum of Modern Art
Unidentified event [Art Strike?]
Art Strike action and demonstration at Museum of Modern Art

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a photograph of Jan van Raay, Jean Toche, Jon Hendricks, and Lil Picard, by Tom Lloyd.

did.unittitle: Political information and poster show at Museum

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Note accompanying photographs: "I think it was organized by Farman."

did.unittitle: Stella Waitzkin happening
Public hearing at the New York City Council against the expansion of the Metropolitan Museum of Art into Central Park
Demonstration in support of the Panther 21
William Kunstler and Abbie Hoffman among others at Panther 21 demonstration
Art Workers Coalition (or possibly Art Strike) information action in the Grand Hall of the Metropolitan Museum of Art
Guerrilla Art Action Group and Belgium government in exile, preliminary action to flag burning
Some of the works exhibited at The People's Flag Show, Judson Memorial Church
Some of the works exhibited at The People's Flag Show
A few of the works at The People's Flag Show
Symposium on Repression during the opening of The People's Flag Show at Judson Memorial Church

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photographs of Faith Ringgold, Abbie Hoffman, and Kate Millett.

did.unittitle: Flag burning [People's Flag Show?]
The day following the arrest of the Judson Three

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Note accompanying photographs: "Closing The People's Flag Show, press conference on church steps, meeting at AWC re: arrest, etc."

did.unittitle: Reading of a statement of support by the Rev. Howard Moody and moderator Anna Lou Pickett of Judson Church for the Judson Three
[Art Workers Coalition panel on the Judson Three]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Panelists depicted include Florynce Kennedy and Gerald Lefcourt, attorneys defending the Judson Three, as well as lawyer Robert Projansky, art critic Barbara Rose, MoMA Director of Public Information Elizabeth Shaw, New York Council on the Arts Visual Program Director Allon Schoener, and gallery owners Richard Feigen and Howard Wise.

did.unittitle: Flag burning ceremony by Judson Three and their supporters just after the first trial hearing
Collecting petitions in the lobby of the Museum of Modern Art to change laws regarding the flag
[Jean] Toche and [Jon] Hendricks gathering signatures on petition and making a public statement in the lobby of the Museum of Modern Art
Art Workers Coalition demonstration for free day of admission to the Museum of Modern Art
Art Workers Coalition in the Metropolitan Museum of Art (cockroach action?)
Art Workers Coalition action in the Metropolitan Museum of Art during a meeting of its Board of Trustees

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Note accompanying photographs: "leaflets and cockroaches were distributed."

did.unittitle: Press conference of the Judson Three and their supporters
[Demonstration in support of the Judson Three]
Demonstration by members of the art community in support of the Judson Three
Art Workers Coalition protest at Guggenheim Museum of Art re: censorship of [Hans] Haacke exhibition by director Tom M. Messer
Professional and Administrative Staff Association of the Museum of Modern Art strike against the museum
PASTA MoMA strike
PASTA MoMA strike [Art Workers Coalition demanding removal of And Babies poster on exhibit]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes images of museum security guards removing Jean Toche from the building.

did.unittitle: Art Workers Coalition demonstration at the Museum of Modern Art against Nelson Rockefeller the Butcher of Attica

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Among the protesters depicted are Alice Neel, and Faith Ringgold and her daughter Michele Faith Wallace.

did.unittitle: Multi-media event at Douglas College?

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Some photographs are dated April 14, 1970, although they appear to depict the same event.

did.unittitle: Women in the Arts meeting?
Charlotte Moorman's Avant Garde Festival
Guerrilla Art Action Group action/benefit for Attica at the Avant Garde Festival

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Image of John Lennon and Yoko Ono.

did.unittitle: Guerrilla Art Action Group Attica action during Avant Garde Festival
Lil Picard performance in Elenore Lester's (?) class in Drama at New York University
"Are Museums Relevant to Women?" public hearing at the Brooklyn Museum of Fine Art

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Among the speakers depicted are Faith Ringgold and Lucy Lippard.

did.unittitle: Virginia and Jean Toche and Jon Hendricks at his home at #1 White Street, New York City
Lottery drawing at Castelli Gallery at the artists' benefit for the New York Civil Liberties Union

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes an image of Faith Ringgold, Jean Toche, and Jon Hendricks. Note accompanying photographs: "The drawing of the lottery tickets at the Leo Castelli Gallery. This was the finale of the Artists' Benefit for the New York Civil Liberties Union which was done in part because of their defense of the Judson 3 and Radich flag cases."

did.unittitle: Jan van Raay performance: "Metamorphotos" at 9th annual Avant Garde Festival
Guerrilla Art Action Group Papers

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of photocopies of correspondence, manifestos, action scripts, internal memoranda, interview transcripts, press releases, open letters, clippings, and other materials pertaining to the Guerrilla Art Action Group. Included is a cover page signed by GAAG co-founder Jon Hendricks on May 16, 1974, indicating the dossier is incomplete. The papers, which date from 1969 to 1974, document the group's advocacy for artists' rights and attempts to enact museum reforms in New York, largely through protests, demonstrations, and marches at MoMA and the Metropolitan Museum of Art. The series contains materials related to an anti-war protest staged in front of Picasso's Guernica at MoMA, a demonstration supporting creation of a study center for Black and Puerto Rican art at the museum, and one of the group's first actions, a demonstration demanding the removal of Rockefeller family members from the MoMA board. Also documented is The People's Flag Show, an exhibition that resulted in the arrests of Faith Ringgold and GAAG co-founders Jean Toche and Hendricks. Additionally, the series contains letters written on behalf of other artists and activists, such as Jan van Raay, feminist art historian Cindy Nemser, and Black activist Angela Davis; and a copy of Left Curve magazine, inscribed by Toche and Hendricks.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Guerrilla Art Action Group
bioghist.p: The Guerrilla Art Action Group (GAAG) was founded in New York City in 1969 by Jon Hendricks, Poppy Johnson, and Jean Toche. The group organized or participated in a number of actions in support of artists' rights, civil rights, and women's rights and in protest of the Vietnam War, censorship, corporate influence in museums, and New York State policies regarding poverty and prisons. Like the Art Workers Coalition, GAAG's goals included expanding representation of Black and Puerto Rican artists in New York museums, and the two groups often joined in demonstrations such as the Art Strike against Racism, Sexism, Repression, War. GAAG was active until the mid-1970s.

did.unittitle: Guerilla Art Action Group documents 1969-1974 (incomplete)

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The Guerrilla Art Action Group (GAAG) was founded in New York City in 1969 by Jon Hendricks, Poppy Johnson, and Jean Toche. The group organized or participated in a number of actions in support of artists' rights, civil rights, and women's rights and in protest of the Vietnam War, censorship, corporate influence in museums, and New York State policies regarding poverty and prisons. Like the Art Workers Coalition, GAAG's goals included expanding representation of Black and Puerto Rican artists in New York museums, and the two groups often joined in demonstrations such as the Art Strike against Racism, Sexism, Repression, War. GAAG was active until the mid-1970s.
